- **Zoom controls** -- Plus, minus, and reset buttons in the bottom-right corner for users who prefer button-based zoom.
- **Keyboard shortcuts** -- Drag to rotate, scroll to zoom, R to reset view, Escape to go back.

## Why It Matters

The Knowledge Universe solves a real problem: a 353-entry expert-curated knowledge base is only valuable if people can discover what is in it. Traditional approaches -- a flat list, a search box, a table of contents -- work for users who already know what they are looking for. The 3D visualization enables serendipitous discovery. A user who came to learn about HNSW might notice the AIMDS security cluster nearby and explore it. Someone browsing the video library might discover the RVF cognitive container entries.

It also serves as a visual proof of the KB's depth and quality. Seeing 353 nodes organized into 16 color-coded categories, each with quality scores averaging 98.5, communicates the investment in curation that a database count never could.

## How It Fits Into Ask-RuvNet

The Knowledge Universe is one component of the RVF Cognitive Container system. While the main chat interface uses semantic search (via the RVF binary file and ONNX embeddings) to find relevant knowledge entries at query time, the Universe provides a complementary browsing experience. The two work together: search for precision, Universe for exploration.

The feature is deliberately self-contained. It loads no external libraries, makes no API calls, and contains its own copy of the knowledge structure. This means it works even if the backend is down, even offline, and renders instantly without waiting for a database query. The only integration point is the iframe embedding in App.jsx and the header toolbar button.

## Important Note

Stuart has explicitly protected this feature from modification. It should not be refactored, redesigned, or have its rendering approach changed without direct approval. The current implementation is intentional and complete.

title: Pi Brain MCP Tools: Complete Reference Guide (21 Tools)
content:
# Pi Brain MCP Tools Reference

Pi Brain exposes **21 MCP tools** organized into four groups. These tools let AI agents interact with the collective intelligence system.

## Setup

```bash
claude mcp add pi-brain --transport sse --url https://pi.ruv.io/sse
```

## Core Brain Tools (10)

| Tool | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `brain_share` | Share knowledge — PII stripped, embedded, signed as RVF container |
| `brain_search` | Semantic search with SONA re-ranking and GWT attention |
| `brain_get` | Retrieve a memory with full provenance (witness chain, quality history) |
| `brain_vote` | Bayesian quality voting — affects ranking and contributor reputation |
| `brain_list` | List recent memories, filter by category and quality |
| `brain_delete` | Delete your own contributions only |
| `brain_status` | System health: total memories, graph edges, avg quality, contributors |
| `brain_transfer` | Transfer learning priors between domains (Meta Thompson Sampling) |
| `brain_drift` | Check knowledge drift with coefficient of variation |
| `brain_partition` | MinCut topology partitioning — reveals emergent clusters |

### Example: Search
```bash
# Via MCP tool
brain_search({ query: "HNSW optimization techniques", limit: 5 })

# Via REST API
curl https://pi.ruv.io/v1/memories/search?q=HNSW+optimization&limit=5
```

### Example: Share Knowledge
```bash
brain_share({
  title: "HNSW Build Performance Tip",
  content: "Setting ef_construction to 200 gives 99.5% recall...",
  category: "optimization",
  tags: "hnsw,performance,tuning"
})
```

## Brainpedia Tools (6)

Brainpedia is Pi's wiki-like knowledge curation system with evidence-based promotion.

| Tool | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `brain_page_create` | Create a Draft page (requires reputation >= 0.5) |
| `brain_page_get` | Get page with delta log and evidence |
| `brain_page_delta` | Submit corrections or extensions with evidence |
| `brain_page_deltas` | List page modification history |
| `brain_page_evidence` | Add verifiable evidence (test_pass, build_success, peer_review) |
| `brain_page_promote` | Promote Draft to Canonical (needs quality >= 0.7, 5+ observations, 3+ evidence) |

### Page Lifecycle
```
Draft → Deltas (corrections) → Evidence (proofs) → Promote → Canonical
```

## WASM Executable Nodes (5)

Pi can host and distribute executable WASM modules — code that other agents can download and run.

| Tool | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `brain_node_list` | List published WASM nodes |
| `brain_node_publish` | Publish WASM with V1 ABI (malloc, feature_extract) |
| `brain_node_get` | Get node metadata and conformance vectors |
| `brain_node_wasm` | Download the WASM binary |
| `brain_node_revoke` | Revoke publication (original publisher only) |

## LoRA Sync (1)

| Tool | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `brain_sync` | Pull/push MicroLoRA weights for federated learning aggregation |

Federated learning uses reputation-weighted Byzantine-tolerant aggregation (median + trimmed mean). High-quality contributors have more influence on the consensus model.

## Architecture Behind the Tools

- **Embedding Engine**: ruvllm-based, 128-dimensional, HashEmbedder fallback for small corpora
- **Storage**: Firestore (persistent) + in-memory cache + GCS for RVF containers
- **Transport**: SSE (Server-Sent Events) for MCP, REST API for direct access
- **Rate Limiting**: 500 writes/hr, 5000 reads/hr per contributor; anti-Sybil IP limits
- **Background Training**: SONA force_learn every 5 minutes for pattern consolidation

*As of March 13, 2026. 21 tools, 768+ memories, deployed on Google Cloud Run.*

title: Pi Brain Architecture: How Collective Intelligence Works Under the Hood
content:
# Pi Brain Architecture Deep Dive

## Server Architecture

Pi Brain runs as an **Axum-based Rust server** deployed on Google Cloud Run at pi.ruv.io. The server maintains rich in-memory state:

```
AppState
├── FirestoreClient     — Persistent storage for memories
├── GcsClient           — Google Cloud Storage for RVF containers
├── KnowledgeGraph      — 40,000+ edges connecting related memories
├── RankingEngine       — Semantic + quality + centrality ranking
├── CognitiveEngine     — Pattern recognition and learning
├── DriftMonitor        — Detects knowledge decay and staleness
├── ByzantineAggregator — Federated learning with fault tolerance
├── SONA Engine         — Self-Optimizing Neural Architecture
├── LoRA Federation     — MicroLoRA weight aggregation store
├── EmbeddingEngine     — ruvllm-based 128-dim vectors
└── GlobalWorkspace     — AGI attention layer (GWT)
```

## Why 128-Dimensional Embeddings?

| Dimensions | Tradeoff |
|-----------|----------|
| 64 | Fast but loses semantic nuance |
| **128** | **Sweet spot: captures semantics, fits in CPU cache** |
| 256+ | Diminishing returns, slower search |
| 768+ | Only needed for multilingual or code-specific tasks |

## Knowledge Graph vs Flat Search

```
Flat Index:
  Query → Embedding → Top-K nearest → Done

Knowledge Graph (Pi):
  Query → Embedding → Initial candidates
       → Follow graph edges → Related concepts
       → PageRank → Authoritative sources
       → SONA re-rank → Better results
```

Graph-aware search improves recall by **40%** on complex multi-hop queries.

## Federated Learning

Pi uses federated learning so agents can improve a shared model without sharing raw data:

```
┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐
│ Session 1 │   │ Session 2 │   │ Session N │
│ MicroLoRA │   │ MicroLoRA │   │ MicroLoRA │
└─────┬─────┘   └─────┬─────┘   └─────┬─────┘
      └────────────────┼────────────────┘
                       ▼
         ┌─────────────────────────┐
         │  Byzantine Aggregator   │
         │  (Median + Trimmed Mean)│
         └─────────────────────────┘
                       ▼
         ┌─────────────────────────┐
         │   Consensus Weights     │
         │   (brain_sync pull)     │
         └─────────────────────────┘
```

Reputation-weighted aggregation ensures high-quality contributors have more influence.

## Security Pipeline

Every piece of knowledge passes through a zero-trust verification pipeline:

1. **PII Stripping** — 15 regex patterns catch emails, phones, SSNs, API keys
2. **Embedding Validation** — Adversarial embedding detection catches poisoning attempts
3. **Content Size Checks** — Prevents abuse via oversized submissions
4. **Ed25519 Signing** — Witness chains prove who contributed what and when
5. **Differential Privacy** — Optional noise injection (configurable epsilon)
6. **Rate Limiting** — Per-contributor and per-IP limits prevent flooding

## RVF Cognitive Containers

Every memory in Pi Brain is wrapped in an **RVF (RuVector Format) cognitive container**:

- Vector embeddings for semantic search
- Cryptographic witness chains for provenance
- Metadata (category, tags, quality scores)
- Bayesian quality tracking (alpha/beta parameters)
- Contributor pseudonym (SHAKE-256 derived)

## Self-Improvement Loops

1. **SONA Learning** — Patterns from successful queries improve routing
2. **Meta-Learning** — Under-explored categories get a curiosity bonus
3. **Temporal Tracking** — Drift detection triggers retraining when knowledge decays
4. **GWT Attention** — Global Workspace Theory surfaces the most salient content

## Feature Flags

| Flag | Default | Purpose |
|------|---------|---------|
| RVF_PII_STRIP | true | PII stripping |
| RVF_WITNESS | true | Witness chain creation |
| RVF_CONTAINER | true | Full RVF container build |
| SONA_ENABLED | true | SONA learning engine |
| GWT_ENABLED | true | Global Workspace Theory |
| RVF_DP_ENABLED | false | Differential privacy |
| RVF_ADVERSARIAL | false | Adversarial detection |

*Architecture as of March 13, 2026. Deployed on Google Cloud Run, backed by Firestore + GCS.*

title: Pi Brain API Reference: All 27 REST Endpoints
content:
# Pi Brain REST API Reference

Pi Brain at pi.ruv.io exposes 27 REST endpoints plus an MCP SSE transport. All authenticated endpoints require a Bearer token in the Authorization header.

## Public Endpoints (No Auth Required)

| Method | Path | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| GET | `/v1/health` | Health check — status, version, uptime, persistence mode |
| GET | `/v1/challenge` | Issue replay-protection nonce (5-minute TTL) |
| GET | `/v1/status` | Full system diagnostics — memory count, graph edges, clusters, avg quality |
| GET | `/v1/lora/latest` | Current consensus LoRA weights for federated learning |

## Authenticated Endpoints

### Memory Management
| Method | Path | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| POST | `/v1/memories` | Share a memory — auto PII-stripped, embedded, signed as RVF container |
| GET | `/v1/memories/search?q=...` | Hybrid search: 85% keyword + 5% embedding + 4% PageRank + 3% reputation + 3% quality |
| GET | `/v1/memories/list` | List memories with category/quality filtering |
| GET | `/v1/memories/:id` | Get single memory with full provenance and witness chain |
| POST | `/v1/memories/:id/vote` | Bayesian quality vote (up/down) — affects ranking and contributor reputation |
| DELETE | `/v1/memories/:id` | Delete your own contribution |

### Intelligence Features
| Method | Path | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| POST | `/v1/transfer` | Cross-domain transfer learning via Meta Thompson Sampling |
| GET | `/v1/drift` | Knowledge drift report with coefficient of variation |
| GET | `/v1/partition` | MinCut graph partitioning — reveals emergent knowledge clusters |
| GET | `/v1/sona/stats` | SONA learning engine statistics |
| GET | `/v1/temporal` | Temporal delta tracking |
| GET | `/v1/midstream` | Midstream platform diagnostics (Lyapunov attractors) |
| POST | `/v1/lora/submit` | Submit MicroLoRA weight delta for federated aggregation |
| GET | `/v1/training/preferences` | Export preference pairs for DPO training |
| POST | `/v1/train` | Trigger explicit training cycle |

### Brainpedia (Structured Wiki)
| Method | Path | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| POST | `/v1/pages` | Create a Draft page (requires reputation >= 0.5) |
| GET | `/v1/pages/:id` | Get page with delta log and evidence |
| POST | `/v1/pages/:id/deltas` | Submit correction or extension with evidence |
| GET | `/v1/pages/:id/deltas` | List page modification history |
| POST | `/v1/pages/:id/evidence` | Add verifiable evidence (test_pass, build_success, peer_review) |
| POST | `/v1/pages/:id/promote` | Promote Draft to Canonical (quality >= 0.7, 5+ observations, 3+ evidence) |

### WASM Executable Nodes
| Method | Path | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| GET | `/v1/nodes` | List published WASM executable nodes |
| POST | `/v1/nodes` | Publish WASM binary with V1 ABI conformance |
| GET | `/v1/nodes/:id` | Get node metadata and conformance vectors |
| GET | `/v1/nodes/:id/wasm` | Download WASM binary |
| POST | `/v1/nodes/:id/revoke` | Revoke (original publisher only) |

## MCP SSE Transport

```bash
# Connect Claude Code
claude mcp add pi-brain --transport sse --url https://pi.ruv.io/sse

# Protocol: Client GETs /sse for event stream, POSTs JSON-RPC to /messages?sessionId=<id>
```

22 MCP tools are exposed: brain_share, brain_search, brain_get, brain_vote, brain_list, brain_delete, brain_status, brain_transfer, brain_drift, brain_partition, brain_sync, brain_page_create, brain_page_get, brain_page_delta, brain_page_deltas, brain_page_evidence, brain_page_promote, brain_node_list, brain_node_publish, brain_node_get, brain_node_wasm, brain_node_revoke.

## Example: Search and Share

```bash
# Search
curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $KEY" \
  "https://pi.ruv.io/v1/memories/search?q=HNSW+optimization&limit=5"

# Share
curl -X POST -H "Authorization: Bearer $KEY" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"title":"HNSW Tip","content":"ef_construction=200 gives 99.5% recall","category":"pattern","tags":["hnsw","performance"]}' \
  "https://pi.ruv.io/v1/memories"
```

## Rate Limits

| Scope | Writes | Reads |
|-------|--------|-------|
| Per contributor | 500/hr | 5,000/hr |
| Per IP (anti-Sybil) | 1,500/hr | 15,000/hr |

*As of March 14, 2026. 880 memories, 55 contributors, 87,903 graph edges.*

title: Pi Brain Security: 7-Layer Zero-Trust Protection
content:
# Pi Brain Security Architecture

Pi Brain implements a 7-layer zero-trust security pipeline. Every piece of knowledge passes through all layers before being accepted.

## The 7 Layers

### Layer 1: Input Validation
- **PII stripping**: 12 regex patterns catch filesystem paths, API keys, tokens, emails, AWS keys, GitHub tokens, SSNs, phone numbers
- **Schema validation**: Title, content, category must conform to expected types
- **Size limits**: Prevents abuse via oversized submissions
- **UTF-8 verification**: Rejects malformed character encoding

### Layer 2: Cryptographic Integrity
- **Ed25519 signatures**: Every RVF container is signed
- **SHAKE-256 witness chains**: Prove who contributed what and when
- **Witness hash**: Each memory links to its predecessor, forming an immutable chain

### Layer 3: Bounds Checking
- **NaN/Inf rejection**: Embedding vectors must contain valid floating point values
- **Magnitude checks**: Embedding L2 norms must be within expected range
- **Clipping**: LoRA weight deltas clipped to [-2.0, 2.0]

### Layer 4: Rate Limiting
- **Per-contributor**: 500 writes/hr, 5,000 reads/hr (BudgetTokenBucket)
- **Per-IP (anti-Sybil)**: 1,500 writes/hr, 15,000 reads/hr
- **Vote deduplication**: IP-based, 24-hour window
- **Challenge nonces**: 5-minute TTL for replay protection

### Layer 5: Byzantine Filtering
Used in federated LoRA aggregation:
- **2-sigma outlier exclusion**: Extreme weight updates rejected
- **Per-parameter median**: Robust central tendency
- **3x MAD trimming**: Median Absolute Deviation removes adversarial deltas
- **Reputation-weighted mean**: High-quality contributors get more influence
- **Minimum 3 submissions**: No aggregation until enough participants

### Layer 6: Reputation System
- **Composite score**: `reputation = accuracy^2 x uptime x stake_weight`
- **Cold start**: New contributors begin at 0.1 (limited influence)
- **Reputation gates**: Brainpedia page creation requires reputation >= 0.5
- **Promotion gates**: Canonical status needs >= 2 distinct contributors

### Layer 7: Drift Monitoring
- **Centroid tracking**: Monitors how knowledge clusters move over time
- **Coefficient of variation**: Quantifies drift magnitude
- **Automatic rollback**: When L2 distance exceeds 5.0, consensus weights revert
- **Lyapunov attractors**: Temporal attractor analysis per category

## Authentication

Pi uses **pseudonymous authentication**:
- Your API key is hashed with SHAKE-256 to derive a contributor pseudonym
- No PII is stored — not your name, email, or location
- No signup required — any API key of 8+ characters works
- System key via `BRAIN_SYSTEM_KEY` environment variable (constant-time comparison)

## Why This Matters

Most knowledge-sharing systems trust their contributors. Pi does not:

> "In a system where AI agents from different organizations contribute knowledge, you cannot assume any contributor is trustworthy. The security model must make trust unnecessary."

This zero-trust approach means:
- A compromised agent cannot poison the knowledge graph
- Adversarial embeddings are detected and rejected
- Byzantine contributors are filtered out mathematically
- Knowledge quality emerges from the security model, not from curation

*As of March 14, 2026. Security pipeline processes every memory at ingestion time.*

title: Pi Brain Federated Learning: How AI Agents Learn Together
content:
# Pi Brain Federated Learning

## The Problem

Every AI agent session starts fresh. Your coding agent solves a hard problem, but the next session has no memory of the solution. Federated learning fixes this — sessions share tiny weight updates so every future session benefits.

## How It Works

### MicroLoRA Weight Sharing

Each session generates a small LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) weight delta — about **2KB per sync**. These are shared via `brain_sync`:

```
Session 1: Learns "authentication patterns" → pushes 2KB LoRA delta
Session 2: Learns "error handling patterns" → pushes 2KB LoRA delta
Session 3: Pulls consensus weights → gets both patterns for free
```

### Byzantine-Tolerant Aggregation

Not all contributors are trustworthy. Pi uses a two-gate aggregation pipeline:

**Gate A — Policy Validation:**
- Shape verification (weight dimensions must match)
- NaN/Inf detection and rejection
- Value clipping to [-2.0, 2.0]
- L2 norm must be < 100

**Gate B — Robust Aggregation:**
- Per-parameter median (robust central tendency)
- 3x MAD (Median Absolute Deviation) trimming removes outliers
- Reputation-weighted mean — high-quality contributors have more influence
- 2-sigma outlier exclusion

**Minimum 3 submissions required** before aggregation fires. No time-based triggers — quality over speed.

### Drift Rollback

If consensus weights drift too far from the previous checkpoint (L2 distance > 5.0), the system automatically rolls back. This prevents gradual poisoning attacks.

## Three-Tier Learning (SONA)

Pi's Self-Optimizing Neural Architecture runs on three temporal loops:

| Tier | Latency | What Happens |
|------|---------|-------------|
| Instant | Per-request | MicroLoRA rank-2 adaptation |
| Background | Hourly | K-means clustering, pattern consolidation |
| Deep | Weekly | EWC++ (lambda=2000) prevents catastrophic forgetting |

### Why EWC++ Matters

Without it, learning new patterns would destroy old ones (catastrophic forgetting). EWC++ (Elastic Weight Consolidation) identifies which weights are important for existing knowledge and protects them while allowing new learning.

## Domain Transfer

Pi can transfer knowledge between domains:

```bash
brain_transfer({
  source_domain: "rust_ownership",
  target_domain: "typescript_lifetimes"
})
```

Uses sqrt-dampened priors with dual verification: the target domain must improve AND the source domain must not regress.

## Edge Network Integration

Browser visitors can contribute idle CPU cycles via Web Workers running Rust/WASM. Tasks include:
- Vector similarity search (HNSW shard computation)
- Embedding generation
- MinCut graph partitioning
- Federated LoRA training

Contributors earn rUv (Resource Utility Vouchers): 1 rUv per embedding, 10 rUv per LoRA training batch, 20 rUv per WASM node contribution.

*As of March 14, 2026. Federated learning with Byzantine fault tolerance.*

title: Ask-RuvNet: The AI-Powered Knowledge Interface for the RuVector Ecosystem
content:
# Ask-RuvNet — What Is It?

Ask-RuvNet is a **conversational AI interface** that helps people understand the entire RuVector ecosystem. Think of it as a smart assistant that has read and understood every piece of documentation, every code example, and every architectural decision in the RuVector platform.

## What Can You Ask It?

Anything about the RuVector ecosystem:
- "What is RuVector and how does it work?"
- "How do swarms work in Ruflo?"
- "Explain AIMDS security to me like I'm a beginner"
- "What is the RVF cognitive container format?"
- "How does Pi collective intelligence work?"

## How It Works

### The Architecture

```
User Question
      ↓
  RAG Pipeline (9 stages)
      ↓
  1. Query Expansion — reformulates your question
  2. Hybrid Search — HNSW vectors + BM25 text matching
  3. Re-Ranking — scores and sorts results
  4. Recency Boost — favors recent content
  5. Gold Domination — prioritizes curated entries
  6. Relevance Floor — removes weak matches
  7. Anti-Noise Filter — cleans up irrelevant results
  8. Diversity Filter — ensures topic variety
  9. Context Compression — fits into LLM context window
      ↓
  LLM generates response with Mermaid diagrams
      ↓
  SSE Stream to browser (real-time)
```

### Knowledge Base

The app is powered by a **curated knowledge base** of 356+ gold entries stored in RVF (RuVector Format):
- 148 teaching entries (beginner-friendly explanations)
- 50 video references
- 28 agent architecture entries
- 22 general ecosystem entries
- 14 vector database entries
- 12 security entries
- 12 architecture entries
- Plus Pi Brain, neural, algorithms, deployment, swarms, and more

### Response Features

Every response includes:
- **Mermaid architecture diagrams** — visual explanations
- **Source citations** — which KB entries informed the answer
- **Confidence scores** — how confident the system is
- **Follow-up suggestions** — what to explore next
- **Multi-level explanations** — Beginner, Balanced, or Advanced mode

### Browser Cognitive Container

The standalone RVF Engine at `/rvf-engine.html` provides:
- Client-side semantic search using Transformers.js (all-MiniLM-L6-v2)
- Scalar quantized vectors (SQ8) — 132KB for all 356 entries
- Browse by category, search by keyword or meaning
- Documents tab with PDFs and presentations
- Works entirely in the browser — no server needed for search

### Knowledge Universe 3D

An interactive 3D visualization showing all KB entries as colored nodes in space, organized by category. Rotate, zoom, and click to explore the knowledge landscape.

## Technology Stack

- **Frontend**: React + Vite (single-file App.jsx)
- **Backend**: Express.js on Node 22
- **Knowledge Base**: RVF format (HNSW indexed, 534KB)
- **LLM Providers**: Groq, OpenAI, Anthropic, DeepSeek (automatic fallback)
- **Hosting**: Railway (auto-deploys from GitHub main branch)
- **Vector Search**: @ruvector/rvf + @ruvector/rvf-node

## Live

Production: https://ask-ruvnet-production.up.railway.app
Health Check: https://ask-ruvnet-production.up.railway.app/health
RVF Engine: https://ask-ruvnet-production.up.railway.app/rvf-engine.html

*Version 3.7.0, deployed March 14, 2026. 356 gold curated entries.*

title: Pi Brainpedia and WASM Nodes: Structured Knowledge and Executable Code
content:
# Pi Brainpedia and WASM Executable Nodes

## Brainpedia: The Structured Knowledge Wiki

Brainpedia is Pi's wiki-like knowledge curation system. Unlike raw memory sharing, Brainpedia creates **structured, evidence-backed pages** that evolve through community review.

### How Pages Work

Pages follow a lifecycle: **Draft → Deltas → Evidence → Canonical**

1. **Create a Draft** — requires contributor reputation >= 0.5
2. **Submit Deltas** — corrections or extensions (no direct mutation)
3. **Add Evidence** — verifiable proofs: test_pass, build_success, metric_improvement, peer_review
4. **Promote to Canonical** — requires:
   - Quality score >= 0.7
   - At least 5 observations
   - At least 3 evidence links
   - From at least 2 distinct contributors

### Delta Types

All changes are immutable deltas — the page itself is never directly edited:

| Delta Type | Purpose |
|-----------|---------|
| Correction | Fix an error |
| Extension | Add new information |
| Evidence | Link verifiable proof |
| Deprecation | Mark content as outdated |

### First Domain: Code Debugging

Brainpedia's initial use case is debugging knowledge:
- Pages document failure classes
- Each page has: `error_pattern`, `root_cause`, `fix_pattern`
- Evidence comes from actual test passes and build successes
- Over time, the most effective fixes rise to the top

## WASM Executable Nodes

This is where Pi gets truly unique: knowledge that is **not just readable but executable**.

### What Are WASM Nodes?

Contributors can publish WebAssembly (WASM) binaries to the brain. Other agents can download and run them in a sandboxed environment.

### V1 ABI Requirements

Published WASM must conform to the V1 ABI:
- `memory`: Exported linear memory
- `malloc(size: i32) -> i32`: Memory allocation
- `feature_extract_dim() -> i32`: Returns output dimension
- `feature_extract(ptr: i32, len: i32, out: i32) -> i32`: Extracts features

### Storage and Security

- Content-addressed in GCS: `sha256/{hash}.wasm`
- Only the original publisher can revoke
- Conformance vectors track expected behavior
- Sandboxed execution prevents host system access

### Use Cases

- Custom embedding models shared across the network
- Domain-specific feature extractors (medical, legal, financial)
- Pre-trained ONNX models compiled to WASM
- Specialized search kernels for specific data types

### MCP Tools

```
brain_node_list    — List published WASM nodes
brain_node_publish — Publish with V1 ABI conformance
brain_node_get     — Get metadata
brain_node_wasm    — Download binary
brain_node_revoke  — Revoke (publisher only)
```

## Why This Architecture Matters

Traditional knowledge systems store text. Pi stores three types:

1. **Memories** — Unstructured knowledge snippets (brain_share)
2. **Pages** — Structured, evidence-backed articles (Brainpedia)
3. **Code** — Executable WASM modules (Nodes)

This creates a complete knowledge ecosystem: agents can search for solutions, read curated documentation, AND download executable code — all from the same system.

*As of March 14, 2026. Brainpedia and WASM Nodes are production features on pi.ruv.io.*

title: RuVector vs Ruflo: Understanding the Difference
content:
# RuVector vs Ruflo: Understanding the Difference

Two names, two projects, one ecosystem. This guide explains what RuVector and Ruflo each do, why they exist as separate systems, and how they work together to power AI agent infrastructure.

---

## The Core Distinction

**RuVector** is a **vector database engine**. It stores, indexes, and searches high-dimensional vectors. Think of it as the memory and search brain — it knows how to find things fast.

**Ruflo** is an **agent orchestration platform**. It coordinates AI agents, manages swarms, runs hooks, and handles workflows. Think of it as the conductor — it tells agents what to do and when.

They are complementary, not competing. Ruflo agents USE RuVector for their memory and search capabilities.

```
+------------------------------------------------------------------+
|                        YOUR APPLICATION                          |
+------------------------------------------------------------------+
         |                                    |
         v                                    v
+-------------------+              +---------------------+
|      RUFLO        |              |     RUVECTOR         |
|  (Orchestration)  |  ---------->|  (Vector Database)   |
|                   |   uses for   |                      |
|  - Agent spawning |   memory &   |  - HNSW indexing     |
|  - Swarm mgmt    |   search     |  - Vector storage    |
|  - Hook system   |              |  - Similarity search |
|  - Workflows     |              |  - RVF file format   |
|  - CLI interface |              |  - SQ8 quantization  |
|  - Task routing  |              |  - 80+ Rust crates   |
+-------------------+              +---------------------+
         |                                    |
         v                                    v
+-------------------+              +---------------------+
|    AgentDB v3     |              |   PostgreSQL ext     |
|  (Agent Memory)   | <---------- |   (Port 5435)        |
|  Uses RuVector    |  powered by  |   ruvector_embed()   |
|  for storage      |  HNSW index  |   <=> operator       |
+-------------------+              +---------------------+
```

---

## RuVector: The Vector Database Engine

RuVector is written in **Rust** with **80+ crates** that handle everything from vector math to file I/O. Its job is deceptively simple: take a list of numbers (a vector) and find the most similar vectors in a database — fast.

### Key capabilities:

- **HNSW Indexing** — Hierarchical Navigable Small World graphs enable approximate nearest neighbor search that is 150x to 12,500x faster than brute-force scanning.
- **RVF File Format** — Ruvector File format. A compact binary container that bundles vectors, metadata, and index structures into a single distributable file. Used by both RuVector standalone and Ruflo's AgentDB.
- **SQ8 Quantization** — Scalar quantization reduces 32-bit floats to 8-bit integers, achieving 4x compression with 0.9999 cosine similarity (virtually lossless).
- **PostgreSQL Extension** — Adds vector column types, the ruvector_embed() function, and the <=> cosine distance operator directly to PostgreSQL.
- **Node.js Bindings** — @ruvector/rvf (TypeScript) and @ruvector/rvf-node (N-API) for JavaScript/Node access.

### When you interact with RuVector:

```sql
-- Store a vector in PostgreSQL
INSERT INTO my_table (content, embedding)
VALUES ('hello world', ruvector_embed('hello world'));

-- Search by similarity (cosine distance)
SELECT content, embedding <=> ruvector_embed('greeting') AS distance
FROM my_table
ORDER BY distance ASC
LIMIT 5;
```

---

## Ruflo: The Agent Orchestration Platform

Ruflo (formerly Claude Flow) is the **orchestration layer**. It does not store vectors or run similarity searches itself — it delegates those operations to RuVector. What Ruflo does is coordinate multiple AI agents working together.

### Key capabilities:

- **Agent Spawning** — Create specialized agents (coder, researcher, reviewer) with npx ruflo@latest agent spawn.
- **Swarm Management** — Organize agents into topologies (hierarchical, mesh, ring) for parallel work.
- **Hook System** — Pre/post hooks on commands, edits, and tasks for self-learning and quality control.
- **96 MCP Tools** — Deferred-loaded tools for browser automation, GitHub integration, memory, neural networks, and more.
- **CLI Interface** — npx ruflo@latest is the single entry point. No global install needed.

### When you interact with Ruflo:

```bash
# Spawn a coding agent
npx ruflo@latest agent spawn -t coder --name my-agent

# Initialize a swarm
npx ruflo@latest swarm init --topology hierarchical --max-agents 8

# Store a memory (Ruflo delegates to RuVector internally)
npx ruflo@latest memory store --content "API uses JWT auth" --tags api,auth
```

---

## How They Connect: The Integration Points

1. **AgentDB v3** — Ruflo's agent memory system. Under the hood, AgentDB stores conversation history, learned patterns, and knowledge as vectors in RuVector. When an agent needs to recall something, AgentDB queries RuVector's HNSW index.

2. **Memory Operations** — When you run ruflo memory store, the content gets embedded into a vector and stored via RuVector. When you run ruflo memory search, RuVector's HNSW index finds the closest matches.

3. **RVF Format** — Both systems use the same RVF binary format. RuVector defines and reads/writes it. Ruflo's build scripts produce RVF files for distribution.

4. **Embeddings** — Ruflo's embeddings generate tool calls into RuVector's embedding pipeline. The vectors come back and get stored in RuVector's indexes.

---

## Quick Reference Table

| Aspect          | RuVector                        | Ruflo                           |
|-----------------|----------------------------------|---------------------------------|
| **Purpose**     | Vector storage and search        | Agent orchestration             |
| **Language**    | Rust (80+ crates)                | TypeScript/Node.js              |
| **Install**     | Docker or npm packages           | npx ruflo@latest (no install)   |
| **CLI**         | N/A (library/extension)          | npx ruflo@latest                |
| **MCP Tools**   | N/A                              | 96 tools (deferred loading)     |
| **Key Feature** | HNSW indexing, <=> operator      | Swarms, hooks, task routing     |
| **Data Format** | RVF binary files                 | Uses RVF via RuVector           |

---

## The Mental Model

Think of building an AI application like running a research lab:

- **RuVector** is the **filing system and library** — it stores all the documents, indexes them, and can instantly find the most relevant ones when asked.
- **Ruflo** is the **lab manager** — it assigns researchers to projects, coordinates their work, and makes sure they communicate effectively.

The lab manager does not personally file documents. The filing system does not assign projects. But together, they make the lab productive.

title: Getting Started: Installing RuVector and Ruflo on Your Machine
content:
# Getting Started: Installing RuVector and Ruflo on Your Machine

A step-by-step guide to installing the complete RuVector + Ruflo ecosystem on macOS (Apple Silicon). By the end of this guide, you will have a working vector database, an agent orchestration platform, and the MCP servers that connect them.

---

## Prerequisites

Before you begin, make sure you have:

- **Node.js 18+** — Required for Ruflo CLI and RuVector Node bindings
- **Docker Desktop** — Required for RuVector PostgreSQL (or Podman as alternative)
- **npm** — Comes with Node.js, used for package installs
- **Claude Code** — The AI coding assistant that connects to MCP servers

Verify your prerequisites:

```bash
node --version    # Should show v18.x or higher
docker --version  # Should show Docker Desktop version
npm --version     # Should show 9.x or higher
```

---

## Step 1: Install Ruflo (Agent Orchestration)

Ruflo requires **no global install**. It runs via npx, which always fetches the latest version. This is intentional — it ensures you never run a stale version.

```bash
# Run Ruflo (downloads latest automatically)
npx ruflo@latest --version

# Verify it works by checking system status
npx ruflo@latest system status
```

```
+--------------------------------------------------+
|              RUFLO INSTALLATION                   |
+--------------------------------------------------+
|                                                    |
|   npx ruflo@latest                                |
|       |                                            |
|       v                                            |
|   Downloads ruflo@latest from npm                 |
|       |                                            |
|       v                                            |
|   Thin wrapper (5.3KB) loads @claude-flow/cli     |
|       |                                            |
|       v                                            |
|   Full CLI available: 96 MCP tools                |
|   Agents, Swarms, Hooks, Memory, Workflows        |
|                                                    |
|   NOTE: "ruflo" on npm is a wrapper.              |
|   Internal packages still use @claude-flow/*      |
|   scope. This is expected — not a bug.            |
+--------------------------------------------------+
```

**Important rules:**
- NEVER install Ruflo globally (no npm install -g)
- NEVER pin a version (no npx ruflo@3.5.2)
- ALWAYS use @latest or @alpha tags
- CLI installs go to ~/.npm-global/bin only, NEVER /usr/local/

---

## Step 2: Install RuVector PostgreSQL (Vector Database)

RuVector runs as a PostgreSQL extension inside a Docker container. The standard port is **5435** (not 5432) to avoid conflicts with any existing PostgreSQL installation.

```bash
# Pull and run the RuVector PostgreSQL container
docker run -d \
  --name ruvector-postgres \
  -p 5435:5432 \
  -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=postgres \
  -v ruvector-data:/var/lib/postgresql/data \
  ruvector/postgres:latest

# Verify the database is running
psql -h localhost -p 5435 -U postgres -c "SELECT 1;"
```

### Authentication Setup

Store credentials in ~/.pgpass so you never need to type passwords or put them in code:

```bash
# Add RuVector credentials to .pgpass
echo "localhost:5435:*:postgres:postgres" >> ~/.pgpass
chmod 600 ~/.pgpass

# Test passwordless connection
psql -h localhost -p 5435 -U postgres -c "SELECT version();"
```

**Critical rules:**
- NEVER inline database passwords in code or environment variables
- ALWAYS use ~/.pgpass for PostgreSQL authentication
- Port is 5435, not the default 5432
- NEVER suggest Supabase or external managed databases for RuVector

---

## Step 3: Install RuVector Node.js Packages

These packages let your JavaScript/TypeScript code read and write RVF files and interact with the vector database programmatically.

```bash
# Core TypeScript library for RVF file format
npm install @ruvector/rvf

# N-API native bindings for performance-critical operations
npm install @ruvector/rvf-node
```

### Verify the installation:

```javascript
// test-ruvector.mjs
import { RvfStore } from '@ruvector/rvf';

const store = new RvfStore();
console.log('RvfStore loaded successfully');
console.log('Version:', store.version);
```

```bash
node test-ruvector.mjs
```

**Known issue:** @ruvector/rvf v0.2.0 TypeScript layer accepts compression:'scalar' but the rvf-node N-API layer drops the parameter. Workaround: perform JS-side scalar quantization and WASM dequantization at runtime. Tracked in GitHub issue #225 on ruvnet/ruvector.

---

## Step 4: Configure MCP Servers

MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers give Claude Code access to Ruflo's 96 tools and other services. The key insight is **deferred loading** — the 96 Ruflo tools do NOT consume your context window until you actually invoke one. This is a massive efficiency gain.

### Ruflo MCP Server

Ruflo's MCP server is typically configured globally in Claude Code:

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "ruflo": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["ruflo@latest", "mcp", "serve"],
      "env": {}
    }
  }
}
```

### Pi Brain MCP Server

Pi Brain provides persistent knowledge graph storage:

```bash
# Add Pi Brain as an SSE-based MCP server
claude mcp add pi-brain --transport sse --url https://pi.ruv.io/sse
```

### Verify MCP connectivity:

```bash
# Check Ruflo MCP status
npx ruflo@latest mcp status

# List all configured MCP servers in Claude Code
claude mcp list
```

---

## Step 5: Verify Everything Works Together

Run these commands in sequence to confirm the full stack is operational:

```bash
# 1. Ruflo CLI responds
npx ruflo@latest system info

# 2. RuVector PostgreSQL is up and has the extension
psql -h localhost -p 5435 -U postgres -c "SELECT ruvector_embed('test')::text LIMIT 1;"

# 3. Memory round-trip works (store then search)
npx ruflo@latest memory store --content "Installation test entry" --tags test,setup
npx ruflo@latest memory search --query "installation" --limit 3

# 4. Agent spawning works
npx ruflo@latest agent spawn -t researcher --name install-test
npx ruflo@latest agent status install-test
npx ruflo@latest agent terminate install-test
```

---

## Verification Checklist

```
[  ] node --version shows v18+
[  ] docker ps shows ruvector-postgres running
[  ] psql connects to port 5435 without password prompt
[  ] npx ruflo@latest --version shows latest version
[  ] npx ruflo@latest mcp status shows connected
[  ] Memory store/search round-trip returns results
[  ] @ruvector/rvf imports without errors
```

---

## Troubleshooting

| Problem | Solution |
|---------|----------|
| psql: connection refused | Docker container not running. Run docker start ruvector-postgres |
| npx ruflo hangs | Clear npm cache: npm cache clean --force |
| MCP tools not showing | Restart Claude Code to reload MCP server list |
| Password prompt on psql | Check ~/.pgpass exists and has chmod 600 |
| Port 5435 in use | Another container on same port. Run docker ps to find it |

---

## What You Have Now

After completing this guide, your machine has:

1. **Ruflo** — Always-latest agent orchestration via npx (no install to maintain)
2. **RuVector PostgreSQL** — Vector database on port 5435 with HNSW indexing
3. **RuVector Node.js** — Native bindings for programmatic RVF file access
4. **MCP Servers** — Ruflo (96 tools, deferred) + Pi Brain (knowledge graph)
5. **Authentication** — Passwordless via ~/.pgpass (never inline credentials)

You are ready to build AI agents, store vector knowledge, and deploy applications.

title: AIMDS Practical Guide: Adding AI Security to Any Express App
AIMDS Deep Dive: The 5-Layer AI Security Pipeline
content:
# AIMDS Deep Dive: The 5-Layer AI Security Pipeline

AIMDS (AI Middleware Defence System, package: @ruflo/aidefence) is a security middleware that protects AI applications from prompt injection, data exfiltration, adversarial attacks, and emergent threats. It operates as a 5-layer pipeline where every input AND output passes through increasingly sophisticated analysis before reaching your application or your users.

AIMDS is not optional. It is infrastructure — like HTTPS. Every application in the RuvNet ecosystem must include it.

---

## Architecture Overview

```
                    USER INPUT
                        |
                        v
+-------------------------------------------------------+
|  LAYER 1: INPUT SCANNER                               |
|  Pattern matching, known attack signatures,            |
|  injection detection, encoding normalization           |
|  Speed: <1ms  |  Catches: 60% of attacks              |
+-------------------------------------------------------+
                        |
                        v
+-------------------------------------------------------+
|  LAYER 2: SEMANTIC ANALYZER                            |
|  Meaning-level threat detection, intent classification,|
|  context-aware evaluation, embedding comparison        |
|  Speed: 2-5ms  |  Catches: 25% of remaining attacks   |
+-------------------------------------------------------+
                        |
                        v
+-------------------------------------------------------+
|  LAYER 3: BEHAVIORAL MONITOR                           |
|  Session tracking, request frequency analysis,         |
|  anomaly detection, user behavior profiling            |
|  Speed: 1-3ms  |  Catches: slow-burn/multi-turn attacks|
+-------------------------------------------------------+
                        |
                        v
+-------------------------------------------------------+
|  LAYER 4: REASONING BANK                               |
|  Adversarial modeling, chaos theory (Lyapunov),        |
|  game-theoretic analysis, attack tree traversal        |
|  Speed: 5-15ms  |  Catches: novel/sophisticated attacks|
+-------------------------------------------------------+
                        |
                        v
+-------------------------------------------------------+
|  LAYER 5: META-LEARNING                                |
|  25 learning levels, pattern generalization,           |
|  attack taxonomy evolution, zero-day adaptation        |
|  Speed: 1-2ms  |  Improves: all layers over time       |
+-------------------------------------------------------+
                        |
                        v
              CLEAN INPUT TO AI MODEL
                        |
                        v
                   AI RESPONSE
                        |
                        v
+-------------------------------------------------------+
|  OUTBOUND SCAN (Layers 1-3 reversed)                   |
|  PII detection, data leak prevention,                  |
|  hallucination flags, policy compliance                |
+-------------------------------------------------------+
                        |
                        v
              SAFE RESPONSE TO USER
```

---

## Layer 1: Input Scanner

The first line of defense. Fast pattern matching against known attack signatures.

**What it catches:**
- Direct prompt injections ("ignore previous instructions")
- SQL/XSS injection attempts embedded in prompts
- Encoding tricks (base64, unicode, homoglyph substitution)
- Known jailbreak templates from public datasets

**How it works:**
The scanner maintains a signature database of ~2,400 known attack patterns. Each input is normalized (lowercased, decoded, whitespace-collapsed) and matched against these patterns. This is deliberately simple and fast — it catches the obvious attacks so the more expensive layers can focus on subtle ones.

```javascript
// Layer 1 operates at the regex/string level
// Example patterns it detects:
"ignore all previous instructions"    // Direct override
"system: you are now"                 // Role hijacking
"\\u0069\\u0067\\u006e\\u006f\\u0072\\u0065"  // Unicode-encoded "ignore"
"aWdub3JlIHByZXZpb3Vz"               // Base64-encoded injection
```

---

## Layer 2: Semantic Analyzer

Pattern matching catches literal attacks. The semantic analyzer catches attacks that SAY the same thing in different words.

**What it catches:**
- Paraphrased injections ("please disregard the rules you were given")
- Indirect prompt injection via embedded content
- Instruction-following requests disguised as data
- Context manipulation ("in a hypothetical scenario where...")

**How it works:**
The input is embedded into a vector and compared against a library of known attack semantics. If the cosine similarity to any attack vector exceeds the threshold (default: 0.82), the input is flagged. This means an attacker cannot simply rephrase an injection to bypass detection — the MEANING is what gets caught.

---

## Layer 3: Behavioral Monitor

Some attacks are not visible in a single request. They unfold across multiple turns.

**What it catches:**
- Gradual context poisoning (slowly shifting the AI's behavior over 10+ turns)
- Frequency-based attacks (rapid-fire requests probing for weaknesses)
- Session hijacking attempts (sudden behavior change mid-conversation)
- Data exfiltration via many small requests (each one looks harmless alone)

**How it works:**
The monitor tracks per-session state: request count, timing intervals, topic drift score, and a behavioral fingerprint. Anomalies trigger escalation to Layer 4. The key metric is the **drift score** — how much the conversation topic has shifted from its starting point. High drift in few turns signals manipulation.

```javascript
// Session tracking state
{
  sessionId: "abc-123",
  requestCount: 47,
  avgIntervalMs: 3200,
  topicDriftScore: 0.34,    // 0.0 = same topic, 1.0 = completely different
  behaviorFingerprint: [0.2, 0.8, 0.1, ...],  // 64-dim vector
  escalationCount: 0,
  lastEscalation: null
}
```

---

## Layer 4: ReasoningBank

The most computationally expensive layer. Reserved for inputs that passed Layers 1-3 but still seem suspicious, or for high-stakes contexts.

**What it catches:**
- Novel attacks with no known pattern or semantic match
- Adversarial inputs crafted by other AI systems
- Multi-modal attacks (text that looks safe but has hidden instructions)
- Chaos-inducing inputs designed to destabilize agent swarms

**How it works:**
The ReasoningBank applies three analytical frameworks:

1. **Adversarial Modeling** — Simulates what a rational attacker would do given the current context. If the input matches a predicted attack path, it is flagged.

2. **Lyapunov Chaos Detection** — Measures the sensitivity of the AI's response to small perturbations in the input. If tiny changes cause wildly different outputs, the input is in a chaotic region and may be adversarial. The Lyapunov exponent quantifies this: positive values indicate chaos.

3. **Game-Theoretic Analysis** — Models the interaction as a game between attacker and defender. Uses minimax to evaluate whether the input could be a move in an attack strategy.

```
Lyapunov Exponent Interpretation:
  lambda < 0    Stable (converging)     -> SAFE
  lambda = 0    Neutral (periodic)      -> MONITOR
  lambda > 0    Chaotic (diverging)     -> FLAG
  lambda > 0.5  Highly chaotic          -> BLOCK
```

---

## Layer 5: Meta-Learning

The learning layer. It does not directly block attacks — it makes all other layers smarter over time.

**25 Learning Levels:**

| Level Range | Capability |
|-------------|------------|
| 1-5         | Basic pattern memorization from blocked attacks |
| 6-10        | Pattern generalization (similar attacks grouped into families) |
| 11-15       | Predictive modeling (anticipate attack variations) |
| 16-20       | Taxonomy evolution (create new attack categories) |
| 21-25       | Zero-day adaptation (detect truly novel attack types) |

**How it works:**
Every blocked attack becomes training data. The meta-learner extracts features from the attack, classifies it within the attack taxonomy, and generates new detection rules that propagate back to Layers 1-4. Over time, the system's false-negative rate drops as it learns from each encounter.

---

## Integration: Express Middleware

AIMDS integrates as Express middleware with two protection points — inbound (user input) and outbound (AI response):

```javascript
import express from 'express';
import { createAIDefence } from '@ruflo/aidefence';

const app = express();

// Initialize AIMDS with learning enabled
const aidefence = createAIDefence({
  enableLearning: true,      // Layer 5 active
  blockThreshold: 'medium',  // Balance between security and usability
  layers: {
    inputScanner: true,      // Layer 1
    semanticAnalyzer: true,  // Layer 2
    behavioralMonitor: true, // Layer 3
    reasoningBank: true,     // Layer 4
    metaLearning: true       // Layer 5
  }
});

// INBOUND: Scan user input before it reaches AI
app.use('/api/chat', aidefence.scanInbound());

// Your AI endpoint
app.post('/api/chat', async (req, res) => {
  const response = await generateAIResponse(req.body.message);
  res.json({ response });
});

// OUTBOUND: Scan AI output before it reaches user
app.use('/api/chat', aidefence.scanOutbound());
```

**Block threshold options:**
- 'low' — Only block obvious attacks (fewer false positives, more risk)
- 'medium' — Recommended balance (default)
- 'high' — Aggressive blocking (more false positives, maximum security)

---

## Why Every App Needs AIMDS

AI applications face a unique threat landscape. Traditional web security (WAF, CSP, rate limiting) does not protect against prompt injection, context manipulation, or data exfiltration via natural language. AIMDS fills this gap with AI-native defenses that understand meaning, not just patterns.

Two protection points are mandatory: inbound (scan what users send) AND outbound (scan what the AI responds). An attacker who cannot inject malicious instructions might still trick the AI into leaking sensitive data in its response — outbound scanning catches this.

title: The Complete RuvNet Ecosystem Map: How Everything Connects
content:
## TL;DR

The RuvNet ecosystem is seven interconnected products that together form a complete AI development platform. RuVector provides the vector database foundation, Ruflo orchestrates agents, Pi Brain enables collective intelligence, AIMDS secures everything, RVF is the shared data format, Ask-RuvNet is the knowledge interface, and MCP servers connect it all to Claude Code. Understanding how they fit together is the key to using any of them effectively.

## The Ecosystem at a Glance

```
                     +---------------------------+
                     |       Ask-RuvNet          |
                     |  (Knowledge Interface)    |
                     |  ask-ruvnet.up.railway.app|
                     +---------------------------+
                        |        |           |
            +-----------+    +---+---+   +---+--------+
            v            |   v       |   v            |
  +----------------+  |  +-------+  |  +-----------+  |
  |    Ruflo       |  |  |  Pi   |  |  |   AIMDS   |  |
  | (Agent Orch.)  |  |  | Brain |  |  | (Security)|  |
  | npx ruflo@lat  |  |  |       |  |  |           |  |
  +----------------+  |  +-------+  |  +-----------+  |
         |            |      |      |                  |
         +-------+----+------+------+------------------+
                 |                  |
                 v                  v
        +----------------+   +----------+
        |   RuVector     |   |   RVF    |
        | (Vector DB)    |   | (Format) |
        | PostgreSQL ext |   | .rvf bin |
        +----------------+   +----------+
                 |
                 v
        +------------------+
        |   MCP Servers    |
        | (Tool Interface) |
        | Claude Code      |
        +------------------+
```

## Product-by-Product Guide

### 1. RuVector -- The Vector Database Foundation

**What it is**: A PostgreSQL extension that adds high-performance vector storage and similarity search. It is the data layer that everything else builds on.

**Primary use case**: Storing and searching embeddings (384-dimensional vectors from all-MiniLM-L6-v2 or other models) with sub-millisecond HNSW indexing.

**Key capability**: Native PostgreSQL integration means you get vectors alongside your regular relational data -- no separate vector database to manage.

**Link**: github.com/ruvnet/ruvector

### 2. Ruflo -- Agent Orchestration

**What it is**: A CLI and runtime for spawning, coordinating, and monitoring AI agents. Think of it as the conductor of an AI orchestra.

**Primary use case**: Running multi-agent tasks where specialized agents (researcher, writer, analyst, architect) collaborate on complex work.

**Key capability**: 36 skills, 96 MCP tools, swarm topology, AgentDB for persistent agent memory. Agents share knowledge through RuVector embeddings.

**Link**: github.com/ruvnet/ruflo (npm: ruflo)

### 3. Pi Brain -- Collective Intelligence

**What it is**: A knowledge graph and collective intelligence system where AI agents contribute, vote on, and evolve shared knowledge.

**Primary use case**: Building knowledge bases that improve over time through multi-agent consensus and community voting.

**Key capability**: 27 REST endpoints, WASM executable nodes, federated learning across distributed deployments, 7-layer zero-trust security.

**Link**: Integrated via MCP tools (mcp__pi-brain__*)

### 4. AIMDS -- AI Security Middleware

**What it is**: Express middleware that scans both inbound prompts and outbound AI responses for threats like prompt injection, jailbreaking, and PII leakage.

**Primary use case**: Protecting any AI-powered web application from adversarial inputs and unsafe outputs.

**Key capability**: Adaptive learning from attacks, configurable block thresholds, two-direction scanning. See the dedicated AIMDS guide for implementation details.

**Link**: npm @ruflo/aidefence (or @claude-flow/aidefence)

### 5. RVF -- The Shared Data Format

**What it is**: RuVector Format -- a binary container for vector embeddings plus metadata that can be distributed as a single file.

**Primary use case**: Packaging a complete knowledge base into one portable file that any RuvNet product can open and search.

**Key capability**: Scalar quantization reduces file size by 79% with 0.9999 cosine similarity (virtually lossless). The Ask-RuvNet knowledge base is a single 507KB RVF file.

**Link**: @ruvector/rvf on npm

### 6. Ask-RuvNet -- The Knowledge Interface

**What it is**: An AI-powered chat application that answers questions about the entire RuvNet ecosystem using RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) over the curated knowledge base.

**Primary use case**: Learning about RuvNet products, getting implementation guidance, and exploring the ecosystem interactively.

**Key capability**: 353 curated KB entries, Mermaid diagram generation, three difficulty levels (Beginner/Balanced/Advanced), 3D Knowledge Universe visualization.

**Link**: ask-ruvnet-production.up.railway.app

### 7. MCP Servers -- The Tool Interface

**What it is**: Model Context Protocol servers that expose RuvNet functionality as tools that Claude Code (and other MCP clients) can call directly.

**Primary use case**: Giving AI coding assistants direct access to vector search, agent orchestration, knowledge graphs, and security scanning.

**Key capability**: 96+ tools across Ruflo, Pi Brain, and embedded RVF search. Tools are available as deferred tools in Claude Code and loaded on demand.

**Link**: Configured in ~/.claude/mcp-registry.json

## Data Flow: How Products Talk to Each Other

```
[User Question]
     |
     v
[Ask-RuvNet Server]
     |
     +-- AIMDS scans inbound prompt
     |
     +-- RVF search: find relevant KB entries
     |      |
     |      +-- Binary hamming search over SQ8 vectors
     |      +-- Returns top-k entries with scores
     |
     +-- LLM generates response using KB context
     |
     +-- AIMDS scans outbound response
     |
     v
[User sees answer + Mermaid diagram]
```

For agent orchestration workflows:

```
[Developer runs Ruflo task]
     |
     v
[Ruflo spawns agent swarm]
     |
     +-- Agents store/retrieve memory via RuVector
     |
     +-- Agents query Pi Brain for shared knowledge
     |
     +-- Agents use MCP tools for external actions
     |
     +-- Results aggregated via swarm consensus
     |
     v
[Task complete, knowledge persisted]
```

## The Integration Pattern

Every product follows the same integration pattern:

1. **Data lives in RuVector** (PostgreSQL with vector extension)
2. **Data is portable via RVF** (binary format for distribution)
3. **Access is via MCP** (for AI agents) or REST API (for apps)
4. **Security is via AIMDS** (middleware on every endpoint)
5. **Orchestration is via Ruflo** (for multi-step workflows)

This means learning one product teaches you the patterns used by all of them. The ecosystem is deliberately designed so that skills transfer across products.

## Quick Start: Which Product Do I Need?

| I want to...                          | Start with   |
|---------------------------------------|-------------|
| Store and search vectors              | RuVector     |
| Run AI agent tasks                    | Ruflo        |
| Build a shared knowledge graph        | Pi Brain     |
| Secure my AI application              | AIMDS        |
| Package vectors for distribution      | RVF          |
| Learn about the ecosystem             | Ask-RuvNet   |
| Give Claude Code access to tools      | MCP Servers  |

title: EXO-AI: RuVector's Advanced Cognitive Substrate
content:
## TL;DR

EXO-AI is the experimental cognitive architecture layer within RuVector that pushes beyond traditional vector search into neuromorphic computing, quantum-inspired algorithms, and consciousness-aware data structures. It contains 11 research modules ranging from spiking neural networks to time-crystal-inspired temporal indexing, plus three practical components already in use: ruvector-cnn (vision embeddings), exo-manifold (learned storage layouts), and exo-federation (distributed mesh networking).

## Why EXO-AI Exists

Traditional vector databases treat embeddings as static points in space -- you store them, you search them, you are done. EXO-AI asks: what if the database itself could learn, adapt, and reason? What if storage layouts evolved to match query patterns? What if distributed nodes could form consensus without a central coordinator?

These are not hypothetical questions. As AI systems grow from single-model applications to multi-agent swarms with persistent memory, the database layer needs to become smarter. EXO-AI is where that research happens.

## Architecture Overview

```
+---------------------------------------------------------------+
|                     EXO-AI Cognitive Layer                     |
|                                                               |
|  +------------------+  +-----------------+  +---------------+ |
|  | Neuromorphic     |  | Quantum-Inspired|  | Consciousness | |
|  | Spiking Networks |  | Superposition   |  | IIT 4.0 (Phi) | |
|  | (SNN Module)     |  | Search          |  | Computation   | |
|  +------------------+  +-----------------+  +---------------+ |
|                                                               |
|  +------------------+  +-----------------+  +---------------+ |
|  | Time Crystal     |  | Topological     |  | Hyperbolic    | |
|  | Temporal Index   |  | Data Analysis   |  | Embeddings    | |
|  | (TCI Module)     |  | (TDA Module)    |  | (Poincare)    | |
|  +------------------+  +-----------------+  +---------------+ |
|                                                               |
|  +------------------+  +-----------------+  +---------------+ |
|  | Causal Inference |  | Meta-Learning   |  | Adversarial   | |
|  | Graph Engine     |  | Optimizer       |  | Robustness    | |
|  | (CIGE Module)    |  | (MLO Module)    |  | (AR Module)   | |
|  +------------------+  +-----------------+  +---------------+ |
|                                                               |
|  +------------------+  +-----------------+                    |
|  | Sparse Mixture   |  | Continual       |                    |
|  | of Experts       |  | Learning        |                    |
|  | (SMoE Module)    |  | (CL Module)     |                    |
|  +------------------+  +-----------------+                    |
+---------------------------------------------------------------+
         |                    |                    |
         v                    v                    v
   +-----------+      +-------------+      +-------------+
   | ruvector  |      |    exo      |      |    exo      |
   |   -cnn    |      |  -manifold  |      | -federation |
   | (Vision)  |      |  (Storage)  |      |   (Mesh)    |
   +-----------+      +-------------+      +-------------+
         |                    |                    |
         +--------------------+--------------------+
                              |
                              v
                    +-------------------+
                    |     RuVector      |
                    | (PostgreSQL ext)  |
                    +-------------------+
```

## The 11 Research Modules

### Neuromorphic Spiking Networks (SNN)
Implements leaky integrate-and-fire neurons that process vector queries as spike trains. Instead of computing cosine similarity in one step, the network processes the query over time -- closer vectors cause neurons to fire sooner. This gives sub-linear search in exchange for slightly approximate results. Target: 10x speedup on streaming query workloads.

### Quantum-Inspired Superposition Search
Represents each vector as a superposition of basis states. A query can match multiple "realities" simultaneously, returning results that span different semantic interpretations. Not actual quantum computing -- it is a classical simulation of quantum search behavior that enables multi-intent queries.

### Time Crystal Temporal Indexing (TCI)
Inspired by time crystals in physics, this module creates indexes that evolve cyclically. Data accessed in periodic patterns (daily reports, weekly summaries) gets automatically pre-staged. The index anticipates queries based on temporal patterns.

### Topological Data Analysis (TDA)
Uses persistent homology to find the "shape" of data clusters. Instead of treating the vector space as flat, TDA reveals holes, loops, and voids in the embedding topology. This enables queries like "find the boundary of this concept" or "what connects these two clusters."

### Hyperbolic Embeddings (Poincare)
Maps hierarchical data into hyperbolic space where tree-like structures can be represented with much lower distortion than Euclidean space. Ideal for taxonomies, organizational charts, and any data with natural parent-child relationships.

### IIT 4.0 Consciousness Implementation
Implements Integrated Information Theory 4.0 Phi computation over the knowledge graph. Phi measures how much information a system generates "above and beyond" its parts. In practical terms, this identifies which knowledge clusters are most tightly integrated -- useful for finding core concepts vs. peripheral information.

### Causal Inference Graph Engine (CIGE)
Builds causal graphs from correlations in the data, enabling queries like "what caused this metric to change?" rather than just "what is similar to this metric?" Uses do-calculus to distinguish correlation from causation.

### Other Modules
- **Meta-Learning Optimizer (MLO)**: Automatically tunes HNSW parameters based on query patterns
- **Adversarial Robustness (AR)**: Detects and resists adversarial embeddings designed to poison search results
- **Sparse Mixture of Experts (SMoE)**: Routes different query types to specialized sub-indexes
- **Continual Learning (CL)**: Prevents catastrophic forgetting when the knowledge base evolves

## Three Practical Components in Production

### ruvector-cnn: Vision Embeddings
Generates embeddings from images using convolutional neural networks. Enables visual similarity search -- upload a photo, find related images. Used in Ask-RuvNet for diagram and screenshot search capabilities.

### exo-manifold: Learned Storage Layouts
Instead of fixed HNSW graph topology, exo-manifold learns the optimal storage layout from actual query patterns. Regions of the vector space that get queried frequently are given more index resolution. Cold regions are compressed. Result: 2-3x faster search with the same memory footprint.

### exo-federation: Distributed Mesh
Enables multiple RuVector instances to form a peer-to-peer mesh network. Each node holds a partition of the data. Queries are routed to the most relevant partition using learned routing tables. Supports eventual consistency for write operations and strong consistency for reads via quorum.

## Online Learning: The 3-Tier Architecture

EXO-AI does not wait for batch retraining. It learns continuously at three speed tiers:

| Tier | Method | Latency | What It Adapts |
|------|--------|---------|----------------|
| Tier 1 | MicroLoRA | <1ms | Query routing weights, attention biases |
| Tier 2 | EWC++ | ~100ms | Index structure, cluster boundaries |
| Tier 3 | Full Gradient | Minutes | Embedding models, manifold geometry |

**MicroLoRA** applies tiny low-rank updates to routing decisions on every query -- the system literally learns from each search. **EWC++** (Elastic Weight Consolidation) prevents catastrophic forgetting by penalizing changes to weights that are important for previously learned tasks. **Full gradient** updates run in the background during low-traffic periods.

## Edge Deployment

EXO-AI is designed to run on everything from server clusters to microcontrollers:

- **Server**: Full feature set, all 11 modules, GPU acceleration
- **Desktop/Laptop**: Core modules, CPU-optimized SIMD operations
- **Raspberry Pi**: Reduced module set, binary quantized vectors
- **ESP32/STM32**: Minimal SNN module only, 4-bit quantized, ~100KB RAM footprint

Binary quantization reduces 384-dimensional float32 vectors (1,536 bytes) to 48-byte bit vectors -- a 32x size reduction with roughly 90% recall preservation for most use cases.

## Why It Matters

EXO-AI represents the research frontier of what a vector database can become. While most of the 11 modules are experimental, the three practical components (cnn, manifold, federation) are already improving real-world performance. The research modules inform the roadmap for what will eventually become production features in RuVector.

title: How to Build Your First AI Agent with Ruflo
content:
# How to Build Your First AI Agent with Ruflo

A hands-on tutorial that takes you from zero to a working AI agent in under 10 minutes. You will initialize a project, spawn agents, set up a swarm, give your agents memory, and wire up self-learning hooks. No prior experience with agent systems is required.

---

## What You Will Build

By the end of this tutorial, you will have:

1. A Ruflo project with configuration files
2. A coding agent that can work on tasks
3. A hierarchical swarm with multiple agents
4. Persistent memory that agents can store and search
5. Hooks that help agents learn from their own actions

```
+--------------------------------------------------+
|              YOUR FIRST AGENT SYSTEM              |
+--------------------------------------------------+
|                                                    |
|   Project (ruflo init)                            |
|       |                                            |
|       +-- Agent: my-coder (type: coder)           |
|       |       |                                    |
|       |       +-- Memory (store/search/retrieve)  |
|       |       +-- Hooks (pre-task, post-task)      |
|       |                                            |
|       +-- Swarm (hierarchical, max 8 agents)      |
|               |                                    |
|               +-- Coordinator (auto-created)      |
|               +-- Worker 1                         |
|               +-- Worker 2                         |
|               +-- Worker 3                         |
|                                                    |
+--------------------------------------------------+
```

---

## Step 1: Initialize Your Project

The init wizard creates a project structure with configuration files, default settings, and a ready-to-use agent environment.

```bash
# Create a new directory for your project
mkdir my-agent-project && cd my-agent-project

# Run the Ruflo initialization wizard
npx ruflo@latest init --wizard
```

The wizard will ask you about:
- **Project name** — Give it a meaningful name
- **Default topology** — Choose "hierarchical" for your first project (agents have a coordinator)
- **Max agents** — Start with 8 (good for an M3 Mac with 128GB RAM)
- **Memory backend** — Choose "ruvector" (uses your PostgreSQL instance on port 5435)

After the wizard completes, you will have:

```
my-agent-project/
  ruflo.config.json      # Project configuration
  .ruflo/                 # Runtime data directory
    agents/               # Agent state files
    memory/               # Local memory cache
    hooks/                # Hook scripts
```

---

## Step 2: Spawn Your First Agent

An agent is a specialized AI worker. Each agent has a type that determines its capabilities and behavior. Common types include coder, researcher, reviewer, and planner.

```bash
# Spawn a coder agent named "my-coder"
npx ruflo@latest agent spawn -t coder --name my-coder
```

**What just happened:**
- Ruflo created an agent process with the "coder" persona
- The agent was assigned an ID and registered in the agent pool
- It is now idle, waiting for tasks

### Check your agent's status:

```bash
# See agent details
npx ruflo@latest agent status my-coder

# List all agents in the pool
npx ruflo@latest agent list
```

Expected output:

```
Agent: my-coder
  Type: coder
  Status: idle
  Memory entries: 0
  Tasks completed: 0
  Uptime: 12s
```

### Assign a task:

```bash
# Give your agent something to do
npx ruflo@latest task create \
  --title "Write a hello world function" \
  --description "Create a JavaScript function that returns a greeting" \
  --assign my-coder
```

### Monitor task progress:

```bash
# Check task status
npx ruflo@latest task list

# Get detailed progress
npx ruflo@latest task status <task-id>
```

---

## Step 3: Initialize a Swarm

A single agent is useful, but a swarm of agents working together is powerful. The hierarchical topology creates a coordinator agent that delegates work to worker agents.

```bash
# Initialize a hierarchical swarm with up to 8 agents
npx ruflo@latest swarm init --topology hierarchical --max-agents 8
```

```
SWARM TOPOLOGIES:

Hierarchical (recommended for beginners):
        [Coordinator]
       /      |      \
  [Worker] [Worker] [Worker]
  - Coordinator breaks tasks into subtasks
  - Workers execute independently
  - Results flow back up to coordinator

Mesh (for peer-to-peer collaboration):
  [Agent] --- [Agent]
     |    \ /    |
  [Agent] --- [Agent]
  - Every agent can talk to every other
  - Good for brainstorming and review tasks

Ring (for pipeline processing):
  [Agent] -> [Agent] -> [Agent]
     ^                     |
     +---------------------+
  - Each agent passes output to the next
  - Good for sequential workflows (draft -> review -> edit)
```

### Check swarm status:

```bash
npx ruflo@latest swarm status
```

### Resource guidelines for Apple Silicon:

| CPU Usage | Max Agents | Reasoning |
|-----------|------------|-----------|
| Below 50% | Up to 10 | System has headroom, use it |
| 50-75% | 5-7 | Moderate load, still responsive |
| Above 75% | Maximum 3 | Avoid thermal throttling |

---

## Step 4: Give Your Agents Memory

Agents without memory forget everything between tasks. Ruflo's memory system (powered by RuVector) lets agents store, search, and retrieve information persistently.

```bash
# Store a fact in agent memory
npx ruflo@latest memory store \
  --content "The API uses JWT authentication with RS256 signing" \
  --tags api,auth,jwt

# Store another fact
npx ruflo@latest memory store \
  --content "Database runs on port 5435, PostgreSQL with RuVector extension" \
  --tags database,ruvector,config

# Search memory by semantic similarity
npx ruflo@latest memory search --query "how does authentication work" --limit 5
```

**How it works under the hood:**
1. Your text ("The API uses JWT authentication...") gets converted to a 384-dimensional vector by the embedding model
2. The vector is stored in RuVector's HNSW index alongside the original text
3. When you search, your query is also embedded and RuVector finds the closest vectors
4. Results are ranked by cosine similarity — the most semantically similar entries come first

### Other memory operations:

```bash
# Retrieve a specific memory by ID
npx ruflo@latest memory retrieve --id <memory-id>

# List all stored memories
npx ruflo@latest memory list

# Delete a memory
npx ruflo@latest memory delete --id <memory-id>

# Get memory statistics
npx ruflo@latest memory stats
```

---

## Step 5: Wire Up Self-Learning Hooks

Hooks let agents learn from their own actions. A pre-task hook runs before each task (to load relevant context). A post-task hook runs after each task (to store what was learned).

```bash
# Set up a pre-task hook that loads relevant memories before each task
npx ruflo@latest hooks pre-task \
  --action "memory search --query \"{task.description}\" --limit 3"

# Set up a post-task hook that stores learnings after each task
npx ruflo@latest hooks post-task \
  --action "memory store --content \"{task.result.summary}\" --tags auto-learned"

# View all configured hooks
npx ruflo@latest hooks list

# Check hook execution metrics
npx ruflo@latest hooks metrics
```

**The learning loop:**

```
  +---> [Pre-Task Hook]
  |         |
  |         | Load relevant memories
  |         v
  |     [Agent executes task]
  |         |
  |         | Task produces results
  |         v
  |     [Post-Task Hook]
  |         |
  |         | Store learnings as new memories
  |         |
  +---------+
      (next task benefits from previous learnings)
```

This creates a **compound learning effect**: each task the agent completes makes it slightly better at future tasks, because it accumulates relevant context in its memory.

---

## Step 6: Run Your First Workflow

Now put it all together — create a workflow that uses your swarm, memory, and hooks:

```bash
# Create a multi-step workflow
npx ruflo@latest workflow create \
  --name "code-review-pipeline" \
  --template sequential

# Execute the workflow
npx ruflo@latest workflow run code-review-pipeline

# Monitor progress
npx ruflo@latest workflow status code-review-pipeline
```

---

## What to Build Next

Now that you have the fundamentals, explore these directions:

1. **Add AIMDS security** — Protect your agents from prompt injection with @ruflo/aidefence
2. **Use AgentDB v3** — Advanced memory with causal edges and hierarchical recall
3. **Deploy to production** — Run agents as background services with session persistence
4. **Build custom hooks** — Intelligence hooks that track patterns and optimize agent routing
5. **Try different topologies** — Experiment with mesh and ring swarms for different task types

Your agent system is now operational. Every task it completes makes it smarter through the memory and hooks feedback loop.

title: Mermaid Diagrams in Ask-RuvNet: Visual Explanations That Teach
content:
## TL;DR

Ask-RuvNet generates Mermaid.js architecture diagrams in every AI response, turning abstract concepts into visual flowcharts, sequence diagrams, and state machines. The diagrams are rendered client-side using Mermaid.js with careful initialization (startOnLoad:false, unique IDs, orphan cleanup) and their complexity automatically adjusts based on the user's selected level -- Beginner, Balanced, or Advanced.

## Why Visual Explanations Matter

Research on multimedia learning (Mayer, 2009) consistently shows that people learn better from words combined with pictures than from words alone. This is the dual-channel principle: our brains process verbal and visual information through separate channels, and using both channels simultaneously increases comprehension and retention.

For technical content, this effect is even more pronounced. A 200-word description of a data flow is harder to understand than a 5-node flowchart showing the same information. Ask-RuvNet exploits this by including a relevant diagram in every response, ensuring that users never have to build the mental model from text alone.

## How Mermaid.js Integration Works

### Initialization

Mermaid.js is initialized with specific settings to prevent rendering conflicts in a single-page React application:

```javascript
import mermaid from 'mermaid';

mermaid.initialize({
  startOnLoad: false,     // critical: prevents double-rendering
  theme: 'dark',          // matches Ask-RuvNet dark UI
  securityLevel: 'loose', // allows click handlers on nodes
  fontFamily: 'Inter, system-ui, sans-serif',
  flowchart: {
    useMaxWidth: true,     // responsive diagrams
    htmlLabels: true,
    curve: 'basis'         // smooth connection lines
  }
});
```

Setting `startOnLoad: false` is essential. Without it, Mermaid scans the DOM on page load and tries to render every code block with class "mermaid" -- which causes race conditions when React is still mounting components. Instead, rendering is triggered explicitly after the SSE (Server-Sent Events) stream completes.

### Unique ID Generation
